question: Does a periodic whole-topic reconstruction hold a topic's concepts better than the same time spent on the ordinary due queue?
why: Whole-text free recall beat rereading 61% vs 40% at one week (Roediger & Karpicke 2006) and beat concept mapping on inference tests (Karpicke & Blunt 2011, Science). Structure-level retrieval is among the best-evidenced study acts there is.
why_not_a_default: Every one of those studies is a SINGLE SESSION with one delayed test. There are ZERO spaced-session studies of structure-level recall — nobody has asked whether reconstructing a topic every few weeks beats reviewing its nodes. And Mayrhofer 2023 (N=230) showed the retrieval-vs-elaboration margin shrinks under matched time-on-task, so the honest comparison must equate MINUTES, not items.
threat_to_validity: TIME-ON-TASK. A reconstruction session that runs longer than the queue session it replaces measures time, not method. Cap both arms to the same minutes and say so in the close.
protocol: queue-only: the ordinary due queue, capped to the session's minutes.

reconstruction: close the notes and rebuild the topic's argument skeleton from memory — which concepts, in what order of necessity, and what each one buys. Graded against the topic's own claims by the blind assessor.
Assignment is at the TOPIC-SESSION grain, not the node grain: session-grain randomization is thinner inference than node-grain, and the design file says so rather than pretending otherwise.
Both arms keep every node's ordinary schedule; the reconstruction replaces the session's activity, never the FSRS state.
